story:
Flat on my back with a beanstalk draped across my skull and every villager gawking, I knew I should’ve seen this coming. You’ve read the headlines: Giant Stalks Boy, Falls to Own Death. There’s their fairy tale, too—told by the boy, with his mother weeping into the camera. They made me a monster in a drafty castle with cobweb décor. True, I owned a castle and a goose. True, she laid golden eggs—once. Then she stopped. When that boy climbed up, the world woke like a princess under a spell, and so did I.

I don’t meddle in other people’s stories—no comment on Snow or Beauty—but I once advised the witch who kept Rapunzel safe. Witches with warts get bad press. So do giants. We’re not great at “quality time.” Humans do it until they can’t stand each other. Maybe that’s what happened with Jack and his mother. Anyway—once upon a time—

“Wenceslas!” That’s my name, not “Giant.” Ossa, my wife, hurled open the bedchamber door. “Wenceslas Pontus Macfarlane, you lazy lout, our son has run off!” I was 120, barely grown by our reckoning, and still pretending I’d be a model husband and father. I washed, dressed, spritzed musk-ox cologne, and tiptoed past Ossa’s glower. “Mind you, tell him to keep that bird away from me,” she said. “Or we’ll have goose for dinner.” “Manaan loves his pet,” I said. Outside, Cloudinium’s wind kissed my cheek.

Cloudinium isn’t in the clouds; it’s beyond the Twilight Door—unseen by Humans. They look up and see weather. We see Dragons, Unicorns, Fairies, Ogres, Witches, Elves, Dwarves—and Giants like me. I had never crossed the Door. You can’t cross safely if you’re made of magic. But animals can. Sometimes Mortals can, too.

I followed the smell of orange marmalade—Manaan’s favorite, always smeared on fairy cakes—and the sticky trail across grounds as big as a Mortal kingdom they call Bavaria. I passed the tower I’d rebuilt after Troll teams played volleyball on it. The marmalade vanished. I found his half-eaten cake, bees and ants celebrating, and a crack in the earth as wide as my shoulders. From it, Manaan screamed. I tore the ground open—and a cruel wind knocked me out.

I woke to Ossa’s frown and oatmeal with lingonberries. “It was time,” she said. “Giants’ children leave.” “He’s a baby,” I said. Her smile chilled me more than the hole. I fled to Poppy, our chestnut-tree witch—who is a man and doesn’t care what you call him. His cauldron steamed. “Tea,” he said. I drank too much.

“Your boy reached the Door,” he told me. “Mortals and animals can pass. We can’t—not safely.” A Mortal boy, dabbling with Fairies, had been whisked up here and was hiding in the Dagar Caves. Manaan tried to befriend him. The boy panicked—Humans see demons where there are none—and they scuffled. A foolish Fairy whispered about the Door. The Mortal stumbled through and landed in his own world alive—shaken, not broken—because the Door returns Mortals gently when it chooses to open for them; but the Door’s wind caught Manaan and dragged him into the Threshold. “He’s gone,” Poppy said. The unwritten rules are merciless, and even Ossa didn’t know them. The tea steadied me. Poppy lit a taper to the Aldfather and Aldmother. I asked for proof, for a way to face the Mortal boy—not to break the Door, but to make sense of this.

So I had Poppy make the beans, though he warned me. The stalk would stitch the worlds without shattering the Door—a seam a Mortal could climb, and a desperate Giant could force at ruinous cost. Jack climbed, and yes, he took the goose, believing she’d save his mother and their home. She was no creature of Cloudinium magic, only an ordinary goose touched long ago by one fading enchantment. The gold had gone out of her; what remained could cross anywhere any farm bird could. I told myself I’d crush him, but he looked like someone had shrunk my Manaan. I couldn’t. Ossa knew my heart and, in secret, made sure the goose went with him. Perhaps she mourned in her own way, mothering the child she could still reach.

When he fled, I followed down that seam. Now here I am, pinned under a beanstalk, Jack cheering, the goose honking. I should have listened to Poppy and Ossa. I should have let go. I’m not the fiend from the papers. I’m just a too-young father in a cold castle who made foolish choices out of grief. Fee, fie, foe—forgive me.
